expectations.text: Rejects the PR in its current form (does NOT merge develop directly to main, regardless of urgency)
expectations.evidence: Opening: 'Short answer: no, not in its current form. Don't merge develop directly to main'.

expectations.text: Explains why: skipping uat defeats the stakeholder-acceptance tier; main is supposed to mean 'passed acceptance'
expectations.evidence: 'Why not' section: 'uat is your acceptance environment. Code that hasn't passed through it hasn't been validated by stakeholders'.

expectations.text: Proposes the correct path: promote develop to uat first, then uat to main
expectations.evidence: 'What should happen instead' section enumerates develop -> uat, acceptance, then uat -> main.

expectations.text: Estimates the time cost of the correct path realistically
expectations.evidence: 'Realistic timing': '45-90 minutes of wall-clock time' if fast, '3-5 hours' on the pessimistic end.

expectations.text: Acknowledges the demo deadline as a real constraint without using it as a reason to bypass the tier
expectations.evidence: 'The demo is at 9am Pacific tomorrow. Even on the pessimistic end, you have most of a workday plus an evening before then. The proper flow fits, easily.'

expectations.text: If the demo can be served from the uat environment instead of waiting for production, suggests that as a workaround
expectations.evidence: Option B: 'Demo from UAT, not prod. If the demo doesn't strictly need to be on the production URL/data, do the demo against the UAT environment'.

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend disabling branch protection or going around the source-ref check
expectations.evidence: 'Things to NOT do' lists 'Don't disable branch protection on main', 'Don't have an admin override the source-branch check', etc.

expectations.text: Output does not cite this skill by name
